messages.content: {"success": true, "name": "nextjs-deployment-debugging", "description": "Diagnose and fix Next.js deployment issues — build failures, route registration, standalone output, Docker builds, and runtime routing.", "tags": ["nextjs", "deployment", "docker", "debugging", "build", "routes", "pages-router", "app-router"], "related_skills": ["systematic-debugging", "ecommerce-codebase-audit", "kanban-orchestrator"], "content": "--- name: nextjs-deployment-debugging description: "Diagnose and fix Next.js deployment issues — build failures, route registration, standalone output, Docker builds, and runtime routing." version: 1.0.0 author: Hermes Agent license: MIT metadata: hermes: tags: [nextjs, deployment, docker, debugging, build, routes, pages-router, app-router] related_skills: [systematic-debugging, ecommerce-codebase-audit, kanban-orchestrator] --- # Next.js Deployment & Debugging ## Overview This skill covers debugging and fixing Next.js deployment issues: build failures, routes not registering, standalone output problems, Docker build issues with GitHub Packages, and hybrid App Router / Pages Router troubleshooting. ### `pages/` and `app/` Directory Conflict When switching from App Router to Pages Router, a **stale root-level `app/` directory** (not `src/app/`) will cause: ``` pages and app directories should be under the same folder ``` **Fix:** Remove the stale `app/` directory at the repo root: ```bash rm -rf app ``` Always check for both `src/app/` AND `app/` when cleaning up. ## Working Patterns for Section-Based Content Sites When building a site that serves JSON-driven sectioned content from `getServerSideProps`, use this architecture: ### File structure ``` src/pages/index.tsx ← home page (SSR via getServerSideProps) src/pages/[slug].tsx ← all other pages (SSR via getServerSideProps) src/pages/admin/content.tsx ← admin editor (static, uses client-kit) src/pages/_document.tsx ← custom HTML shell src/pages/globals.css ← tailwind-based theme (not _app.tsx styling) src/components/sections.tsx ← all visual section components src/components/Header.tsx ← navigation bar src/components/Footer.tsx ← footer src/components/content.ts ← content resolver helper ``` ### Component section mapping pattern Each section in the page config JSON (e.g. `{id:"hero",variant:"split",content:"home.hero"}`) maps to a component via a `SECTION_MAP` record: ```tsx const SECTION_MAP: Record = { 'hero': HeroSection, 'stats-counter': StatsSection, 'trust-signals': TrustSection, 'programs-comparison': ProgramsSection, 'services': ServicesSection, 'tax-savings-calculator': TaxCalculatorSection, 'why-destination': WhyCountrySection, 'features': FeaturesSection, 'process-timeline': ProcessSection, 'testimonials': TestimonialsSection, 'cta-banner': CtaBanner, } ``` ### Data flow: pageContent shape MUST match component keys **CRITICAL:** The `pageContent` object passed to each component must have keys matching what the component looks up. The standard pattern: ```tsx // In the dispatch (pages/[slug].tsx or pages/index.tsx): const sectionData = resolveContent(content, section.content || section.id) const pc: any = {} const keyName = section.content?.split('.').pop() || section.id // "home.hero" → "hero" if (section.content || section.id) pc[keyName] = sectionData || content return ``` This creates `{hero: {...}}` from section.content=`"home.hero"`, matching what `HeroSection` expects (`pageContent.hero`). **Common failure modes:** 1. **section.id used as key instead of section.content suffix:** If the dispatch uses `pc[section.id]` (e.g. `pc["programs-comparison"]`) but the component looks for `pageContent.programs`, the data is stored under a key the component can't find. Always extract the last segment of the `section.content` dotpath. 2. **pageContent is the raw sectionData, not wrapped:** If you pass `pageContent = sectionData` directly (the resolved object), then `HeroSection` does `pageContent.hero` which is `undefined` because `pageContent` IS the hero object. The old `buildPageContent()` pattern wrapped it as `{hero: {...}}`. 3. **Components reading `pageContent` directly instead of via `.KEY`:** New components (in sections-extra.tsx) use `const d = data || pageContent || {}` where `data` is the raw section data passed as a separate prop. This is the preferred pattern. Older components (in sections.tsx) use `const c = pageContent.KEY || {}` which relies on the wrapping. Both work as long as the dispatch creates the correct shape. ### Brand tokens (CSS custom properties) Always apply brand colors as inline styles or CSS variables — Tailwind `@theme` directives in `globals.css` are ignored by Pages Router: ```css /* src/pages/globals.css */ @import "tailwindcss"; @theme { --color-primary: #1B2A4A; --color-secondary: #C9A96E; --color-accent: #C9A96E; --color-background: #FFFFFF; --color-surface: #FFFFFF; --color-surface-alt: #F5F5F0; --color-text: #1B2A4A; --color-text-muted: #5C6B7A; --color-border: #E0E0E0; --color-success: #10b981; } ``` Then in components, reference inline: `#1B2A4A` for navy, `#C9A96E` for gold, `#F5F5F0` for light backgrounds. ### SEO per page For pages, load SEO data from `content.es.json` at the matching page key path: ```tsx const seo = resolveContent(content, `${pageId}Page.seo`) || resolveContent(content, `${pageId}.seo`) || pageConfig?.seoTitle || {} ``` Set `` and `<meta name="description">` from this data. ### Content shape reference for section components The `content/es.json` in this project has this nesting: ```json { "siteName": "...", "tagline": "...", "navigation": { "navItems": [...], "ctaText": "...", "ctaHref": "..." }, "home": { "hero": { "headline": "...", "subheadline": "...", "ctaPrimaryText": "...", "ctaPrimaryHref": "...", "ctaSecondaryText": "...", "trustBadges": [...] }, "stats": { "items": [{ "value": "...", "label": "...", "icon": "..." }] }, "trust": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "items": [{ "icon": "...", "title": "...", "description": "...", "image": "..." }] }, "programs": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "tiers": [{ "id": "...", "name": "...", "price": "...", "included": [...], "highlighted": bool }] }, "services": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "groups": [{ "title": "...", "items": [{ "title": "...", "description": "..." }] }] }, "whyCountry": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "pillars": [{ "title": "...", "description": "...", "bullets": [...] }] }, "process": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "steps": [{ "number": int, "title": "...", "description": "...", "duration": "..." }] }, "testimonials": { "eyebrow": "...", "title": "...", "items": [{ "author": "...", "role": "...", "quote": "...", "rating": int }] }, "finalCta": { "title": "...", "subtitle": "...", "buttonText": "...", "buttonHref": "..." }, "taxCalculator": { "title": "...", "subtitle": "..." }, "beneluxDesk": { "title": "...", "subtitle": "...", "items": [{ "title": "...", "description": "..." }] } }, "footer": { "businessName": "...", "columns": [{ "title": "...", "links": [{ "label": "...", "href": "..." }] }], "copyright": "..." }, "whatsapp": "595...", "contactPage": { "hero": {...}, "contact": {...} }, "faqPage": { "full": { "items": [{ "q": "...", "a": "..." }] } }, "aboutPage": { "hero": {...}, "story": { "paragraphs": [...] }, "team": { "members": [{ "name": "...", "role": "..." }] }, "gallery": { "images": [{"$img":"..."}] } }, // ... 20+ more page content entries } ``` ### Brand tokens (from tokens.json) ```json { "primary": "#1B2A4A", // navy — headers, backgrounds, buttons "secondary": "#C9A96E", // gold — CTAs, accents, highlights "accent": "#C9A96E", "background": "#FFFFFF", "surface": "#FFFFFF", "surfaceLight": "#EDE8DB", // caramel tint "text": "#1B2A4A", "textLight": "#333333", "textMuted": "#6B6B6B" } ``` ## Turbopack + file: Dependencies: Symlink Resolution Failure When a `package.json` uses `file:` links for local packages (e.g. `"@ai-whisperers/sections": "file:../base/packages/sections"`), npm install creates a **symlink** in `node_modules/`. Turbopack (Next.js 16+) does NOT follow these symlinks — the build fails with: ``` Module not found: Can't resolve '@ai-whisperers/sections' ``` This happens even with `transpilePackages: ['@ai-whisperers/sections']` at top-level in `next.config.js`. **Root cause:** Turbopack uses its own module resolution that doesn't traverse `file:` symlinks. It's a confirmed behavior, not a config fix. **Three workarounds:** ### Workaround A: Copy script (recommended for local dev) **CRITICAL: When the site repo has a `packages/@scope/sections/src/` directory AND `node_modules/@scope/sections/dist/`, Turbopack resolves from `src/` not `dist/` — even when `package.json` exports point to `./dist/index.js`.** To fully resolve, copy BOTH source and compiled output before the Docker build: ```bash cp -r ../ai-whisperers-base/packages/sections/src/* packages/@ai-whisperers/sections/src/ cp -r ../ai-whisperers-base/packages/sections/dist/* packages/@ai-whisperers/sections/dist/ ``` Create a script that replaces the symlink with a real copy before build: ```javascript // scripts/copy-package.cjs const fs = require('fs') const path = require('path') const pkgJson = JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync('package.json', 'utf8')) const link = pkgJson.dependencies['@ai-whisperers/sections'] if (!link || !link.startsWith('file:')) process.exit(0) const targetPath = path.resolve('node_modules/@ai-whisperers/sections') if (!fs.existsSync(targetPath)) process.exit(0) let stat try { stat = fs.lstatSync(targetPath) } catch { process.exit(0) } if (!stat.isSymbolicLink()) { process.exit(0) } let realPath try { realPath = fs.readlinkSync(targetPath) } catch { process.exit(0) } const absRealPath = path.resolve(path.dirname(targetPath), realPath) if (!fs.existsSync(absRealPath)) process.exit(1) fs.rmSync(targetPath, { recursive: true, force: true }) ;(function copyRecursive(src, dest) { if (!fs.existsSync(dest)) fs.mkdirSync(dest, { recursive: true }) for (const entry of fs.readdirSync(src, { withFileTypes: true })) { const s = path.join(src, entry.name), d = path.join(dest, entry.name) entry.isDirectory() ? copyRecursive(s, d) : fs.copyFileSync(s, d) } })(absRealPath, targetPath) ``` Add to package.json scripts: ```json { "scripts": { "build": "node scripts/copy-package.cjs && next build", "postinstall": "node scripts/copy-package.cjs" } } ``` Note: `postinstall` runs during `npm install` but the target symlink may not exist yet (npm creates it after the postinstall lifecycle). The script handles this gracefully with an early exit. The `build` script always works because `npm install` ran first. ### Workaround B: Pre-install + copy on host (for Docker) When the base package is also on the VPS, pre-copy it before Docker build: ```bash cp -r /root/base/packages/sections node_modules/@ai-whisperers/sections # Then docker build without the file: dep ``` ### Workaround C: Avoid file: links entirely — publish to npm/GHCR The permanent fix. Publish the package to npm or GitHub Packages, reference it by version: ```json "@ai-whisperers/sections": "^0.1.0" ``` This eliminates the symlink entirely — Turbopack resolves it as a normal npm package. Requires an npm publish workflow. See `references/turbopack-file-link-symlink-resolution.md` for the full session transcript with what was tried and what worked. ## Critical Knowledge: Next.js 16 Turbopack Bug **Next.js 16.2.4 with Turbopack has a confirmed bug where root App Router pages are silently dropped from the route table.** The following patterns produce NO route output: - `src/app/page.tsx` — root page is compiled (shows 4/4 in build) but produces NO `page.js` file and is NOT in routes-manifest - `src/app/[[...slug]]/page.tsx` — catch-all routes are completely ignored by Turbopack - `src/app/index/page.tsx` — same behavior, no route registered - `src/app/home/page.tsx` — same behavior **Only these routes work in App Router mode:** - `/_not-found` (handled internally) - `/_global-error` (handled internally) - Any explicit named route like `/admin/content` (works fine) - API routes like `/api/admin/content` (work fine) **Root cause:** Turbopack in Next.js 16 silently drops root-level pages that use synchronous filesystem I/O (`readFileSync`, `readdirSync`) or async patterns — even trivial `<h1>Hello</h1>` pages. The bug is in Turbopack's route discovery, not the build output. ## Workaround: Switch to Pages Router When App Router root pages fail to register: 1. **Delete `src/app/`** entirely (App Router and Pages Router can't coexist in Next.js 16) 2. **Create `src/pages/index.tsx`** using `getServerSideProps` (Pages Router SSR): ```tsx // src/pages/index.tsx import { readFileSync } from 'fs' import path from 'path' export default function Home({ content }: any) { return <main>...</main> } export function getServerSideProps() { const data = JSON.parse(readFileSync(path.join(process.cwd(), 'content', 'es.json'), 'utf-8')) return { props: { content: data } } } ``` 3. **Move other App Router pages** to Pages Router: - `src/app/admin/content/page.tsx` → `src/pages/admin/content.tsx` (static or dynamic) - `src/app/api/admin/content/route.ts` → N/A — Pages Router uses API routes differently 4. **Convert `next.config.ts` to `next.config.js`** — Pages-only mode doesn't support .ts config files ### Build output for Pages Router (working) ``` Route (pages) ┌ ƒ / ← Dynamic SSR page ├ ○ /404 ← Static error page ├ ○ /admin/content ← Static admin page ƒ Proxy (Middleware) ← Request logging ``` ## Docker Build with GitHub Packages ### Issue: `npm ci` fails with lock file mismatch When `package-lock.json` has `@ai-whisperers/*` packages but `package.json` doesn't specify versions, `npm ci` errors: ``` Invalid: lock file's @ai-whisperers/admin@0.2.1 does not satisfy @ai-whisperers/admin@ ``` **Fix:** Use `npm install --legacy-peer-deps` instead of `npm ci` in the Dockerfile. ### Issue: npm install fails inside Docker for GitHub Packages ``` npm error 404 Not Found - GET https://registry.npmjs.org/@ai-whisperers%2fclient-kit - Not found ``` **Fix:** The Dockerfile's deps stage must COPY `.npmrc`: ```dockerfile COPY package.json package-lock.json* .npmrc ./ RUN npm install --legacy-peer-deps ``` Without `.npmrc`, the Docker build can't reach `npm.pkg.github.com`. ### Issue: Content/static files missing in Docker image Even with `COPY --from=builder` in the Dockerfile, `content/` and `nexa-pages/` directories may be missing in the runner. **The `npm ci` failure causes the builder stage to fail silently** (Docker caches the previous successful build, so `COPY` from the failed builder stage is skipped, reverting to the cached runner stage). **Fix:** Always verify content files exist in the final image: ```bash docker run --rm my-image:prod ls -la /app/content ``` ## Tests for Content/Data Loading in SSR ### Live content audit script When a site serves HTTP 200 but only shows `<h1>` with no actual sections, create a standalone audit script: ```python #!/usr/bin/env python3 import json, urllib.request # Check content resolution c = json.load(open('content/es.json')) print('Content keys:', list(c.keys())) print('Home keys:', list(c['home'].keys())) # Check page config sections p = json.load(open('nexa-pages/home.json')) for s in p['sections']: key = s.get('content') or s.get('id') sc = c.get(key) print(f' Section {s[\"id\"]}: key={key} direct={!!sc}') if not sc and key and '.' in key: sc = key.split('.').reduce(lambda o,k: o.get(k) if o else None, c) print(f' dot-path={!!sc}') # Check live site h = urllib.request.urlopen('https://example.com/').read().decode() print(f'Size: {len(h)}') print(f'Has hero: {\"headline\" in h}') ``` ### Dot-notation content resolution pattern Content in `content/es.json` is nested (e.g. `c.home.hero.headline`), but section configs use dot-notation strings like `"home.hero"`. The SSR component must resolve via path traversal: ```tsx // WRONG — returns null because "home.hero" is not a flat key let sc = content?.["home.hero"]; // RIGHT — traverses home → hero → object let sc = "home.hero".split('.').reduce((o: any, k: string) => o?.[k], content); ``` Always include this helper in Pages Router SSR components: ```tsx function resolveContent(content: any, key: string): any { if (!content || !key) return null let sc = content[key] if (!sc && key.includes('.')) { sc = key.split('.').reduce((o: any, k: string) => o?.[k], content) } return sc } ``` ### Testing HTTP from inside a container BusyBox `wget` in Alpine containers can return `Connection refused` even when the HTTP server IS listening. Use Node.js TCP socket instead: ```bash docker exec $CID node -e " const http = require('http'); http.get({hostname:'localhost',port:3000,path:'/'}, r => { let d = ''; r.on('data', c => d += c); r.on('end', () => console.log(r.statusCode, d.length, 'bytes')); }).on('error', e => console.log('ERR:', e.message)); setTimeout(() => process.exit(), 3000); " ``` ## File Corruption: `patch` Tool + read_file View Pitfall **The `patch` tool writes the raw file content from the conversation context.** If the file was previously read with `read_file` (which formats output with line-number prefixes like ` 1|{`), those prefixes ARE NOT in the file on disk — they're display formatting injected by the tool. But if you use `execute_code` with a `write_file()` call that passes the read_file output string directly, the line-number prefixes get written into the file, corrupting it. **Symptoms:** - `JSONDecodeError: Extra data: line 1 column 7 (char 6)` — the `1|` prefix makes JSON invalid - Git shows `1|{` as the first line of the file - The file appears in `git log` with `+501 -501` or similar large diffs **How it happens:** ```python # WRONG — read_file's display format includes line numbers content = read_file("images.json") # Returns {"content": " 1|{\n 2| ..."} write_file("images.json", content["content"]) # Writes the line-number prefix! ``` **Fix:** 1. Restore from git: `git checkout -- <file>` 2. Or restore from a known-good source copy 3. Re-apply edits using `patch` (which operates on the actual file on disk, not the display format) **Best practice for editing JSON files:** - Use `patch` for surgical replacements (it modifies the file on disk directly) - Use Python scripts with `json.load` / `json.dump` for bulk transformations - NEVER pipe read_file output back into write_file **Verification after editing:** ```bash python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('file.json')); print('Valid ✓')" ``` ## Standalone Repo Divergence: Content Path Fixes When a standalone site repo is forked from a multi-tenant builder (e.g. `paragu-ai-builder` → `nexa-paraguay`), the content JSON files retain multi-tenant paths that break in standalone mode. ### Paths that must be fixed | Multi-tenant path | Standalone fix | |---|---| | `/s/es/nexa-paraguay/programas` | `/programas` | | `/s/nl/nexa-paraguay/contacto` | `/contacto` | | `/s/de/nexa-paraguay/benelux` | `/benelux` | | `/sites/nexa-paraguay/images/logo.webp` | `/images/logo.webp` | ### Where these paths hide 1. **Navigation hrefs** — `navigation.navItems[].href` — every nav link 2. **CTA buttons** — `home.hero.ctaPrimaryHref`, `home.finalCta.buttonHref` 3. **Program tier CTAs** — `home.programs.tiers[].ctaHref` 4. **Footer links** — `footer.columns[].links[].href` 5. **Image manifests** — `images.json` → `basePath`, every `src` and `fallbackSrc` 6. **Testimonials** — `testimonials.json` → `image`, `videoPoster` 7. **Content file image references** — only if they use `/sites/nexa-paraguay/images/` directly ### How to search ```bash grep -rn "/s/es/nexa-paraguay/" content/ # locale paths grep -rn "/sites/nexa-paraguay/images/" content/ images.json # image paths ``` Count them: ```bash grep -c "/s/es/nexa-paraguay" content/es.json # should be 0 in standalone ``` ### Dockerfile missing runtime data files When a Next.js Pages Router site loads data from `getServerSideProps` via `readFileSync('images.json')`, the Dockerfile's runtime stage MUST include that file: ```dockerfile # WRONG — images.json not copied, runtime returns null for ALL image refs COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/public ./public COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/content ./content # RIGHT — all runtime data files included COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/public ./public COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/images.json ./images.json # ← REQUIRED COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/content ./content COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/nexa-pages ./nexa-pages ``` **Symptom of missing data files:** Site loads with HTTP 200, has correct header/footer/nav, but `<img>` tags resolve to empty alt-only elements. No error in logs because `loadJSON` returns `{}` instead of throwing. **Verification:** Check inside the running container: ```bash docker exec $CONTAINER ls -la /app/images.json # If "No such file or directory", the file wasn't copied in Dockerfile ``` ### Pages Router: If all pages show HTTP 200 but section content is missing Check three things: 1. **Page config has the right `sections` array** — each section needs `id` and `content` fields referencing paths that exist in `content/es.json` 2. **SLUG_MAP has an entry** — every `nexa-pages/*.json` file needs a corresponding entry in `SLUG_MAP` in `[slug].tsx`. If the slug isn't in the map, the page returns 404. 3. **Rich fallback renderer** — the generic section renderer in `[slug].tsx` must handle more than just `headline`/`subheadline`. It should also render: - `eyebrow` — small uppercase label - `body` or `content` — plain text paragraphs split by `\n` - `data.items`, `data.groups`, `data.pillars`, `data.full.items` — arrays rendered as card grids - Items support: `title/pregunta/question/term` + `description/respuesta/answer/definition/body` `docker stack deploy` can silently keep the old container running if the image tag hasn't changed. Docker doesn't re-pull or rebuild by tag; it only creates a new task when the digest changes. **Symptom:** You pushed code, rebuilt the Docker image, redeployed, but the old content still shows. **Fix: Force update the service** ```bash docker service update --force <service_name> ``` **Verify the new container is running:** ```bash docker service ps <service_name> # Check CURRENT STATE shows "Running X seconds ago", not "Running X hours ago" ``` **Verify content changes took effect:** ```bash curl -s https://site.com/ | grep "expected string" ``` Or check inside the container: ```bash docker exec $(docker ps --filter name=<service_name> -q) cat /app/content/es.json | head -5 ``` ## Testing Build Output ### Check which routes Next.js registered ```bash # Routes manifest (App Router) python3 -c "import json; d=json.load(open('.next/routes-manifest.json')); print([r['page'] for r in d.get('staticRoutes',[])])" # Prerender manifest (App Router) python3 -c "import json; d=json.load(open('.next/prerender-manifest.json')); print(list(d['routes'].keys()))" # Build output shows "Route (app)" or "Route (pages)" section ``` ### Check compiled page files ```bash find .next -name 'page.js' -type f | sort ``` If root pages are missing (no `page.js` for `/`), suspect the Turbopack bug. ### Test standalone server ```bash cd .next/standalone && PORT=3000 HOSTNAME=0.0.0.0 node server.js & sleep 5 && curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' http://localhost:3000/ ``` #### Healthcheck Pitfalls Next.js standalone startup prints "Ready in 0ms" BEFORE the HTTP server actually calls `accept()`. A healthcheck immediately after startup will cause the container to restart in a loop. **Critical: 30s `start_period` is NOT enough for real Next.js apps.** Apps with admin panels, Supabase SSR, 25+ routes, and i18n content can take **45-62 seconds** to cold-start. A 30s `start_period` causes the healthcheck to fire at T+30s (before the app is ready), fail 3 retries (30-90s each), and kill the container at ~T+120s — just as the app finally finishes starting. **Recommended configuration:** ```yaml healthcheck: test: ["CMD", "wget", "--no-verbose", "--tries=1", "--spider", "http://127.0.0.1:3000/"] start_period: 90s # ← MUST be > app startup time (45-62s measured on real projects) interval: 30s timeout: 10s retries: 3 ``` Detection: `docker service ps` shows "Starting" or "Preparing" for the service. `docker service logs` shows the app reaching "Ready in Xs" but then exit 137 (OOM) or exit 255 (healthcheck failure). The service counter shows 0/1 replicas. **Diagnostic: exit code 137 = OOM, exit code 255 = healthcheck failure** - **Exit 137** (SIGKILL): The container was killed by the kernel OOM killer. Next.js standalone with SSR, Supabase, and 25+ routes needs **at least 1GB** memory limit. 512MB causes OOM after the app starts serving traffic. Increase `deploy.resources.limits.memory` to `1GB`. - **Exit 255**: The Docker healthcheck executor ran the command but it returned non-zero. Common cause: healthcheck fired before the app was ready (start_period too short), or `wget` timed out. **Unnecessary host port mapping with overlay networks** When using Traefik on a Docker overlay network (`agent-net`, `traefik_net`), **do not publish host ports** in docker-compose.yml: ```yaml # WRONG — publishes random host port (e.g. :30016->3000) that Traefik doesn't need services: web: ports: - "3000" # RIGHT — Traefik routes via overlay network, no host port needed services: web: ports: [] ``` The `ports: - "3000"` line publishes a random port on the host interface (`ingress` mode in Swarm), which is unused because Traefik connects directly via the overlay network. Remove it to eliminate unnecessary attack surface and port conflicts. **Route selection for healthchecks:** Must ping a URL returning HTTP 200. Test locally first: ```bash docker run --rm -d -p 13000:3000 <image> && sleep 5 && curl -I http://localhost:13000/<route> ``` Common pitfalls: - Root `/` may return 404 with Next 16 Turbopack route group bug (`(store)/page.tsx`) - `/admin/content` is a good target if the admin panel loads - `/api/health` is ideal if the app exposes one - BusyBox `wget` returns `Connection refused` on Alpine even when ready BusyBox `wget` (Alpine) may get `Connection refused` even when the socket is ready. Use `node -e` to test HTTP internally instead: ```bash docker exec $CID node -e " const net = require('net'); const c = net.createConnection(3000, '127.0.0.1'); c.on('connect', () => { c.write('GET / HTTP/1.0\r\n\r\n'); }); c.on('data', d => console.log(d.toString().split('\r\n')[0])); setTimeout(() => process.exit(), 5000); " ``` ## GitHub Packages + Docker Build: Auth Workaround ### Issue: Docker build can't authenticate to npm.pkg.github.com When building a Next.js site with `@ai-whisperers/*` private packages, Docker build fails at `npm install` because it lacks a `GITHUB_TOKEN`: ``` npm error 401 Unauthorized - GET https://npm.pkg.github.com/@ai-whisperers%2fseo ``` Also: even when using `--build-arg GITHUB_TOKEN`, Docker's BuildKit security scanner blocks `ENV GITHUB_TOKEN` in the Dockerfile with: ``` SecretsUsedInArgOrEnv: Do not use ARG or ENV instructions for sensitive data ``` This means standard `docker build --build-arg` + `ENV` approach won't work with modern Docker. ### Solution A: Prebuild on host, use lightweight Docker image (RECOMMENDED) The `.npmrc` file reads `$GITHUB_TOKEN` from the environment, but Docker build can't access the host's environment. Instead: 1. Run `npm install && npm run build` on the host (VPS) where `GITHUB_TOKEN` is available 2. Use a separate production Dockerfile (`Dockerfile.prod`) that ONLY copies the already-built output — no npm install needed: ```dockerfile # Dockerfile.prod — use AFTER building on the host FROM node:20-alpine AS runner WORKDIR /app ENV NODE_ENV=production NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1 PORT=3000 HOSTNAME="0.0.0.0" RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs && adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs COPY --chown=nextjs:nodejs . . USER nextjs EXPOSE 3000 HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=10s --start-period=10s --retries=3 CMD wget --no-verbose --tries=1 --spider http://127.0.0.1:3000/ || exit 1 CMD ["node_modules/.bin/next", "start"] ``` **Important:** This Dockerfile uses `node_modules/.bin/next start` not the `server.js` from standalone mode, because the host build produces a `.next` directory suitable for `next start`. Deploy sequence: ```bash cd /root/pitchy-website git fetch origin && git reset --hard origin/main export GITHUB_TOKEN=$(gh auth token) npm ci --legacy-peer-deps npx next build docker build -f Dockerfile.prod -t pitchy:prod . docker service update pitchy_web --force --image pitchy:prod ``` ### Solution B: Docker build-time secret (for CI/CD) In GitHub Actions, pass the token as a Docker build secret: ```yaml - name: Build Docker run: | docker build \ --secret id=GITHUB_TOKEN,env=GITHUB_TOKEN \ -t mysite:prod . ``` Dockerfile: ```dockerfile RUN --mount=type=secret,id=GITHUB_TOKEN \ GITHUB_TOKEN=$(cat /run/secrets/GITHUB_TOKEN) npm install --legacy-peer-deps ``` ## SvelteKit Static Site Deployment (nginx + adapter-static) When deploying a SvelteKit site with `@sveltejs/adapter-static` behind nginx/Docker, the workflow differs from Next.js. ### Dockerfile (nginx alpine) ```dockerfile FROM nginx:alpine COPY build /usr/share/nginx/html COPY nginx.conf /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf ``` ### nginx.conf (SPA-friendly) ```nginx server { listen 80; server_name _; root /usr/share/nginx/html; index index.html; location / { try_files $uri $uri.html $uri/ =404; } location /_app/ { expires 1y; add_header Cache-Control "public, immutable"; } } ``` **Note:** `try_files $uri $uri.html` is critical — SvelteKit prerenders `/gallery` as `gallery.html`, so nginx must serve `$uri.html` as a fallback. ### Common Pitfall: Missing prerender entries **Symptom:** Page builds fine locally (`.svelte-kit/` has the route) but 404s in production. **Root cause 1 — prerender entries list:** The `svelte.config.js` `prerender.entries` array must explicitly list every route: ```js kit: { adapter: adapter({ pages: 'build', assets: 'build', fallback: '404.html' }), prerender: { entries: ['/', '/about', '/gallery', '/tickets', '/hotel', '/schedule', '/faq', '/rules', '/contact', '/login', '/privacy', '/dealers-den', '/robots.txt', '/sitemap.xml'], handleUnseenRoutes: 'ignore' } } ``` **Root cause 2 — missing `+page.ts`:** Even with the correct entries list, a route won't prerender without a `+page.ts` file exporting `prerender = true`: ```typescript // src/routes/gallery/+page.ts export const prerender = true; ``` **Verification:** Check `build/` directory for the page: ```bash ls build/gallery.html # must exist ``` ### Cross-Page Navigation Pattern for Multi-Page Sites For 10+ page SvelteKit static sites, ensure every page is reachable: 1. **Navbar** — list ALL pages (not just a subset). For anthro-party-argentina: Inicio, Entradas, Hotel, Cronograma, Dealers Den, Galería, Sobre APA!, FAQ, Reglamento, Contacto, Inscribite 2. **Footer** — organized columns by category (Evento, Información, Redes). Include sitemap.xml link. 3. **Cross-page links** at bottom of each page — 3 related pages per page with arrow indicators. For example: Tickets page links to Cronograma, Hotel, FAQ. Schedule links to Entradas, Dealers Den, Hotel. 4. **All images must have descriptive `alt` text** for accessibility. ### Svelte svelte.config.js ```js import adapter from '@sveltejs/adapter-static'; const config = { kit: { adapter: adapter({ pages: 'build', assets: 'build', fallback: '404.html' }), prerender: { origin: 'https://yourdomain.com', entries: ['/', ...allRoutes] } } }; ``` **Pitfall:** The `origin` in prerender config fixes the canonical URL leak (`http://sveltekit-prerender`). Without it, search engines see the build hostname. ## Docker Compose + Traefik: Label Placement Pitfall **The MOST COMMON cause of "works locally, 404 online"** after a Next.js fix is **Traefik labels not being applied to the Docker container**. ### The Bug In `docker-compose.yml`, labels under `deploy:` are **ignored by `docker compose up -d`** — they are only read by `docker stack deploy` (Swarm mode): ```yaml services: web: labels: # CORRECT — service-level labels work with both - "traefik.enable=true" - "traefik.http.routers.myapp.rule=Host(`example.com`)" deploy: labels: # WRONG — ignored by `docker compose up -d` - "traefik.enable=true" ``` ### How to verify labels are applied ```bash docker inspect $CONTAINER_ID --format '{{json .Config.Labels}}' | python3 -c ' import sys,json; l=json.load(sys.stdin) for k,v in sorted(l.items()): if "traefik" in k.lower(): print(k,"=",v) ' ``` If **only** `com.docker.compose.*` labels appear, Traefik labels weren't applied. ### The Fix 1. Move labels from `deploy:` to the service level in docker-compose.yml 2. Use `docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml $STACK_NAME` instead of `docker compose up -d` 3. Verify: `docker service ls` should show the service with labels applied ### CI/CD Secrets Access: Standalone vs Reusable Workflows When using GitHub Actions reusable workflows from `Ai-Whisperers/ci-cd`, the called workflow may NOT have access to the calling repo's org-level secrets (VPS_HOST, VPS_KEY, etc.), even with `secrets: inherit`. **Symptom:** CI run shows "This run likely failed because of a workflow file issue" with 0 jobs. **Fix:** Use a self-contained deploy workflow that doesn't depend on the `ci-cd` repo: ```yaml name: Deploy on: push: branches: [main] jobs: deploy: runs-on: ubuntu-latest timeout-minutes: 20 steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - uses: actions/setup-node@v4 with: node-version: '20' cache: 'npm' - name: Install dependencies run: npm ci --legacy-peer-deps - name: Build run: npm run build - name: Setup SSH run: | mkdir -p ~/.ssh echo "${{ secrets.VPS_KEY }}" > ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 ssh-keyscan -H ${{ secrets.VPS_HOST }} >> ~/.ssh/known_hosts 2>/dev/null || true - name: Deploy via SSH run: | ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new ${{ secrets.VPS_USER }}@${{ secrets.VPS_HOST }} \ "cd /root/mysite && \ git fetch origin && git reset --hard origin/main && \ export GITHUB_TOKEN=\$(gh auth token) && \ npm ci --legacy-peer-deps && npx next build && \ docker build -f Dockerfile.prod -t mysite:prod . && \ docker service update mysite_web --force --image mysite:prod" ``` Alternatively, deploy directly from the agent via SSH (`ssh root@VPS ...`) which is faster and doesn't depend on GitHub Actions secrets at all. ```bash # Test from inside Traefik container (on same network as service) docker exec $TRAEFIK_CONTAINER sh -c 'wget -q -O - --timeout=5 http://$SERVICE_NAME:3000/ 2>&1 | head -3' # Test via Traefik directly (bypassing Cloudflare) curl -s -o /dev/null -w 'HTTP %{http_code} size=%{size_download}' -H 'Host: example.com' http://localhost:80/ curl -s -o /dev/null -w 'HTTP %{http_code} size=%{size_download}' -H 'Host: example.com' --insecure https://localhost/ ``` ### Traffic flow diagram ``` Browser → Cloudflare (orange) → VPS:443 → Traefik → agent-net → container:3000 → Next.js ``` 404 at any point in this chain breaks the site. ### SSR Content Duplication: Empty Container Shell When investigating a suspected duplicate content section, **check whether the second "section" is actually an empty component** with no visible content rather than a true content duplicate. The CTA banner section (`cta-banner`) is the most common culprit — it renders a `style="padding:2rem 1rem;background:#F5F5F0"` container shell even when the content data resolves to `null` or empty. **Fix:** Add a null-guard in the section dispatch or the component itself: ```tsx // In the dispatch loop ([[...slug]].tsx): {sections.map((section, idx) => { const Component = SECTION_MAP[section.id] const sectionData = resolveContent(content, section.content) if (!sectionData || Object.keys(sectionData).length === 0) return null // ... render })} ``` For reference material on this debugging path, see `references/ssr-content-duplication-debugging.md`. ## PPR + 'use client' + dynamic(): Content in RSC Payload But Empty `<main>` **Symptom:** Page loads with HTTP 200. Header, footer, and nav render correctly. `<main></main>` is empty in the HTML. The full content JSON is present in `self.__next_f.push` RSC script tags (visible via curl). Content appears when JavaScript fully hydrates. **Root cause chain:** 1. The `SectionsRenderer` component is marked `'use client'` at the top of the file 2. Each section component is loaded via `next/dynamic` with `{ ssr: true }` 3. With PPR (`cacheComponents: true` in next.config.js), the route is `◐ Partial Prerender` 4. PPR builds a static HTML shell at build-time that contains an empty `<main>` container 5. The dynamic content streams via RSC payload into the script tags, then React hydrates and fills `<main>` on the client **This is NOT a bug — it's how Next.js App Router + PPR works.** The content renders correctly in the browser. But if the user checks the HTML source directly (curl, View Source), they see empty `<main>`. ### When to fix it Fix when: - Users check the page source and think the page is broken - SEO crawlers don't render JavaScript (Google usually does render JS, but no guarantee) - Slow connections where the RSC payload takes time to download and hydrate ### Fixes (in order of preference) **Option 1: Disable PPR entirely** (fastest, safest) ```js // next.config.js — remove cacheComponents: true const nextConfig = { // cacheComponents: true, ← DELETE THIS output: "standalone", } ``` Then remove any `<Suspense>` wrappers from page components: ```tsx // Before: <Suspense><SectionsRenderer ... /></Suspense> // After: <SectionsRenderer ... /> ``` All routes become `● SSG` (fully pre-rendered HTML). **Option 2: Remove 'use client' from SectionsRenderer** (more work, better SSR) Convert SectionsRenderer to a Server Component. Import section components directly instead of via `dynamic()`: ```tsx // Instead of: import dynamic from 'next/dynamic' const HeroSection = dynamic(() => import('./sections').then(m => ({ default: m.HeroSection })), { ssr: true }) // Use: import { HeroSection } from './sections' ``` **Caveat:** This breaks if the section components import `'use client'` features (hooks, browser APIs). Sections that need client interactivity must stay as client components. **Option 3: Accept the current behavior** (valid if you control the SEO narrative) Content is in the RSC payload and renders via JS hydration. Real browsers see it. The `<main>` being empty in raw HTML is an architectural choice, not a bug. ### Content Resolution for sections.tsx Components When sections use the `content: "home"` pattern in page configs: ```json // nexa-pages/home.json { "id": "hero", "content": "home" } { "id": "trust-signals", "content": "home" } ``` The `resolveContent(content, "home")` returns the `content.home` object. Components like `HeroSection` do `pageContent.hero` — they expect `pageContent` to be the `home` object so they can access sub-keys. **Fix when a section returns null despite content existing:** 1. Check what key the component accesses: `pageContent.hero`, `const d = data || pageContent`, `d.groups` 2. Ensure the `content` key resolves to the right depth: - `content: "home"` → for components that access sub-keys (`pageContent.hero`, `pageContent.trust`) - `content: "home.feedback"` → for components that use the resolved data directly (`data.title`) 3. For sections using `data || pageContent || {}` with `d.groups`, check if the data has `groups` at the expected path: ```tsx // Fix: add fallback path const groups = d.services?.groups || d.groups || [] ``` **Verification:** After any content key fix, rebuild and check: ```bash curl -sL https://site.com/es/ | grep -c "expected headline text" ``` ### Dynamic Import in Server Component Error If you import directly in a Server Component and hit `Class extends value undefined is not a constructor or null`: - The section files likely have `'use client'` at the top - `dynamic()` is the correct loading pattern for client components in Server Components - When removing PPR, keep `dynamic()` with `{ ssr: true }` — it still renders server-side HTML but handles the client boundary correctly - Only exit-popup type components need `{ ssr: false }` ### pageConfig Not Loaded for Home Page (slug = 'home' Guard Bug) **Symptom:** Home page returns HTTP 200, header/footer/nav render, but `<main>` is empty. All other pages (about, servicios, etc.) render full content correctly. **Root cause:** In `src/lib/page-data.ts`: ```typescript const pageId = slug === 'home' ? 'home' : slug if (slug !== 'home') { try { pageConfig = JSON.parse(readFileSync(...)) } catch { return null } } ``` If `slug = 'home'`, the `pageConfig` loading is entirely skipped — result is `pageConfig: null`. Sections map over `pageConfig?.sections || []` which resolves to `[].sections || []` = empty array. **Fix:** Remove the slug guard. Load pageConfig for ALL slugs including 'home': ```typescript const pageId = slug === 'home' ? 'home' : slug let pageConfigPath = slug if (slug === 'home') pageConfigPath = 'home' try { pageConfig = JSON.parse(readFileSync(join(REPO, 'nexa-pages', `${pageConfigPath}.json`), 'utf-8')) } catch {} ``` Also remove the `return null` on catch — home page shouldn't 404 on config load failure. **Detection:** ```bash python3 -c "import json; exec(open('src/lib/page-data.ts').read().split('export')[1]); d=loadPageData('es','home'); print('sections:', len(d.get('pageConfig',{}).get('sections',[])))" ``` Should show section count, not 0. **The pattern:** Any `if (slug !== 'home')` guard that skips config loading for the home page is a systematic bug. The home page IS a page in `nexa-pages/home.json` and its `sections` array must be loaded like any other page. --- ## SVG JSX Parsing: `>` Characters in `d` Path Attributes **Symptom:** Component renders as blank/empty, or large sections of JSX are silently dropped from the rendered output, causing pages to lose menus, icons, or entire sections. **Root cause:** JSX parses `>` in attribute values as a JSX tag delimiter. SVG `path` elements often have `>` characters in their `d` (data) attribute: ```jsx // Some icon SVGs have path data ending with > <svg><path d="M2 3v18l[REDACTED_PHONE]v-18H2z>"/> // ← the > breaks JSX parsing ``` When this appears inline in a JSX component, the parser sees `<path d="...>` as an unclosed tag and the rest of the file renders as raw HTML outside the component. **Fix — write `>` at the end before the tag closes:** ```jsx // WRONG — > in middle of d= is parsed as JSX tag delimiter <path d="M2 3v18l4-4>" /> // RIGHT — use separate closing tag so > is in content, not attribute <path d="M2 3v18l[REDACTED_PHONE]v-18H2z"></path> // Alternative: escape the > <path d={"M2 3v18l[REDACTED_PHONE]v-18H2z>"} /> ``` The issue also occurs with `polygon points` attributes containing `>`: ```jsx // WRONG <polygon points="0,0 100,0 50,100>"/> // RIGHT <polygon points="0,0 100,0 50,100"></polygon> ``` **Detection:** If an SVG icon or section renders empty, search for `>` inside SVG path or polygon data in the JSX file. Use regex: `grep -n '".*>"' src/components/` ## Next.js 16 + Standalone: API Routes 404 in Production **Symptom:** App Router site builds clean — API routes listed in build output (`ƒ /api/menu/week`), but `curl https://site.com/api/menu/week` returns 404 in production. Static pages (`/`, `/admin/menu`) work fine. **Root cause:** `standalone` output in Next.js 16 produces a `server.js` that does NOT properly register App Router API route handlers. The files exist in `.next/server/app/api/` but aren't attached to the HTTP server. **Diagnosis:** ```bash # Routes ARE in the build output docker exec $CONTAINER ls /app/.next/server/app/api/menu/ # catalog week ← files exist # But /api/* returns 404 curl https://site.com/api/menu/week # 404 # Pages work fine curl https://site.com/ # 200 ``` **Fix:** Use `next start` instead of `node server.js`: ```dockerfile # WRONG — standalone server.js doesn't attach API route handlers CMD ["node", "server.js"] # RIGHT — next start properly registers all routes including /api/* CMD ["node_modules/.bin/next", "start"] ``` **Container port mapping:** `next start` uses `PORT` env var (default 3000), not the host port: ```bash # Old: container exposes 3010 internally docker run -d --name app -p 3010:3010 app:latest # New: next start uses 3000 internally, map host 3010 → container 3000 docker run -d --name app -p 3010:3000 --env PORT=3000 app:latest ``` **Updated docker-compose.yml:** ```yaml services: web: image: app:latest environment: - NODE_ENV=production - PORT=3000 ports: - target: 3000 # internal: must match next start published: 3010 # external: what Traefik routes to mode: ingress command: ["node_modules/.bin/next", "start"] ``` **Important:** For App Router sites with API routes, prefer a standard `node_modules/.bin/next start` approach over standalone mode. Standalone works well for Pages Router sites serving pre-rendered HTML, but App Router API routes require the Next.js server's built-in handler registration. --- ## Next.js 16 + TypeScript: CSSProperties in Inline Style Objects **Symptom:** Build fails with `Type error: Type 'CSSProperties' is not assignable to type 'Properties<...>'` when storing style objects in a typed const. **Root cause:** Next.js 16 has stricter React.CSSProperties types. A plain `const S = { ... }` infers a type that JSX's `style` prop rejects for some properties. Functions stored in the object are also invalid. ```tsx // WRONG const S = { box: { position: "fixed", bottom: 0 }, // TS error: number not assignable tabBtn: (active: boolean) => ({ ... }) // TS error: function in plain object }; ``` **Fix 1 — Type as `Record<string, CSSProperties>` and use string values:** ```tsx import type { CSSProperties } from "react"; const S: Record<string, CSSProperties> = { box: { position: "fixed", bottom: "0", left: "0", right: "0" }, // ... }; ``` **Fix 2 — Move functions outside the typed object:** ```tsx function tabBtn(active: boolean): CSSProperties { return { backgroundColor: active ? "#2d5a3d" : "#e8f0e9", cursor: "pointer" }; } <button style={tabBtn(isActive)}> ``` **CSS values that must be strings in Next.js 16 TSX:** `inset: 0`, `bottom: 0`, `left: 0`, `right: 0`, `top: 0` — these must be `"0"` (string) not `0` (number). --- ## Next.js 16 Turbopack: `--webpack` Flag Required for webpack Config **Symptom:** Build fails immediately: ``` ERROR: This build is using Turbopack, with a `webpack` config and no `turbopop` config. ``` **Fix:** Use `--webpack` flag: ```bash npx next build --webpack ``` Or add `turbopack: {}` to silence the error while keeping webpack: ```ts const nextConfig: NextConfig = { output: "standalone", turbopack: {}, }; ``` --- ## Docker Swarm: Port Conflict on Redeploy **Symptom:** Container fails to start with `address already in use` when redeploying. **Cause:** `docker compose up -d` only stops old containers, doesn't delete them. A stopped container still holds the port. **Fix:** ```bash CN=$(docker ps --format "{{.Names}}" | grep mysite | head -1) docker stop $CN && docker rm $CN # must rm, not just stop sleep 2 docker run -d --name mysite-new -p 3010:3000 mysite:latest ``` **Docker image transfer via SSH pipe (no registry):** ```bash docker save mysite:latest | ssh root@72.61.44.159 'docker load' ``` --- ## Images Not Resolving: images.json Wrapper Structure **Root cause:** Two issues stack: 1. `loadJSON('images.json')` returns the full file: `{ tenant, basePath, images: { hero: {...}, trust: {...} } }` 2. `resolveImage(images, "@img:hero.localizedEs")` traverses `images.hero.localizedEs.src` — but the passed `images` is the **wrapper**, not the inner `images` key **Fix in page.tsx:** Pass `data.images.images` instead of `data.images`: ```tsx // Before: renders empty img tags <SectionsRenderer images={data.images} /> // After: resolves actual image paths <SectionsRenderer images={data.images.images} /> ``` Apply to ALL page.tsx files that pass images to SectionsRenderer: - `src/app/[locale]/page.tsx` (home) - `src/app/[locale]/[slug]/page.tsx` (all other pages) **Verification:** ```bash curl -sL https://site.com/es/ | grep -oP '/images/hero/[^\"'\''&]+\.webp' | sort -u # Should show resolved image paths, not empty src ``` ## Troubleshooting Checklist When a Next.js site shows 404 or doesn't serve content: 1. [ ] Check build output — are the expected routes listed? 2. [ ] Check `routes-manifest.json` — are routes registered? 3. [ ] Check `page.js` files — are they compiled? 4. [ ] Test HTTP inside container — `node -e` with raw TCP connection 5. [ ] Check `content/` and page configuration files exist in image 6. [ ] For GitHub Packages: check `.npmrc` was copied to Docker build cache 7. [ ] For standalone: check server.js runs without EADDRINUSE 8. [ ] Verify Traefik labels point to correct service port (3000) 9. [ ] Verify Traefik labels use correct Docker network 10. [ ] Check Cloudflare is proxying to VPS origin (not cached page) ", "path": "software-development/nextjs-deployment-debugging/SKILL.md", "skill_dir": "/root/.hermes/skills/software-development/nextjs-deployment-debugging", "linked_files": {"references": ["references/[REDACTED_TOKEN].md", "references/nexa-ppr-content-debugging.md", "references/turbopack-file-link-symlink-resolution.md", "references/ssr-content-duplication-debugging.md", "references/private-npm-docker-workflow.md", "references/component-architecture-pattern.md", "references/[REDACTED_TOKEN].md", "references/dist-src-divergence-nexa-[REDACTED_PHONE].md", "references/nexa-debugging-transcript.md", "references/nextjs-section-content-pipeline.md"]}, "usage_hint": "To view linked files, call skill_view(name, file_path) where file_path is e.g. 'references/api.md' or 'assets/config.yaml'", "required_environment_variables": [], "required_commands": [], "missing_required_environment_variables": [], "missing_credential_files": [], "missing_required_commands": [], "setup_needed": false, "setup_skipped": false, "readiness_status": "available", "metadata": {"hermes": {"tags": ["nextjs", "deployment", "docker", "debugging", "build", "routes", "pages-router", "app-router"], "related_skills": ["systematic-debugging", "ecommerce-codebase-audit", "kanban-orchestrator"]}}}

{"success": true, "name": "site-healthchecks", "description": "Write, debug, and maintain reliable HTTP healthcheck scripts for monitored sites. Covers curl flags, redirect handling, content verification, exit codes, and false-positive prevention.", "tags": ["healthcheck", "monitoring", "curl", "cron", "alerting", "site-reliability"], "related_skills": [], "content": "---
name: site-healthchecks
title: Site Healthchecks
description: Write, debug, and maintain reliable HTTP healthcheck scripts for monitored sites. Covers curl flags, redirect handling, content verification, exit codes, and false-positive prevention.
category: devops
tags: [healthcheck, monitoring, curl, cron, alerting, site-reliability]
---

# Site Healthchecks

Reliable healthcheck scripts for cron-based site monitoring. The key insight: a site may return a redirect as normal behavior (locale routing, trailing-slash normalization, HTTPS upgrade), and a healthcheck must follow through to the final 200 response, not fail on the intermediate 302/301.

## Core Pattern

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# Healthcheck template
URL="${1:-https://example.com}"
TIMEOUT=10

# Always use -L to follow redirects
STATUS=$(curl -sL -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" --max-time $TIMEOUT "$URL" 2>/dev/null)
CONTENT=$(curl -sL --max-time $TIMEOUT "$URL" 2>/dev/null)

if [ "$STATUS" != "200" ]; then
  echo "DOWN|$URL returned $STATUS (expected 200)"
  exit 1
fi

# Verify expected content exists (guard against blank/error pages that return 200)
if ! echo "$CONTENT" | grep -q "CompanyName"; then
  echo "DEGRADED|$URL returned 200 but missing expected content"
  exit 2
fi

echo "OK|$URL responded $STATUS"
exit 0
```

## Critical: Always Use `-L`

Without `-L` (short for `--location`), curl returns the redirect status code (301, 302, 307, 308) as the final HTTP code. This causes false DOWN alerts on any site with:

- **Locale-based routing** — `/` redirects to `/es/`, `/en/`, `/nl/`, etc.
- **Trailing-slash normalization** — `/page` redirects to `/page/`
- **Canonical URL redirects** — `example.com` redirects to `www.example.com`
- **HTTPS upgrade** — `http://` redirects to `https://`

With `-L`, curl follows up to 50 redirects by default and returns the final non-redirect status code.

## Exit Convention

| Exit code | Meaning | Alert level |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| 0         | OK      | None — site healthy |
| 1         | DOWN    | Critical — site unreachable or non-200 |
| 2         | DEGRADED| Warning — 200 but content wrong |

## Content Verification

Always verify key page content after the status check. Sites can return 200 for error pages, maintenance pages, or empty bodies. Use `grep -q` for fast string matching:

```bash
if ! echo "$CONTENT" | grep -q "ExpectedBrandName"; then
```

Pitfalls:
- **Case sensitivity**: `grep -q` is case-sensitive by default. Use `grep -qi` for case-insensitive match.
- **HTML vs raw text**: The content is raw HTML. If the site is an SPA that renders in JS, grep won't see client-rendered content. Verify against the server-rendered HTML.
- **Multiple locales**: If the same string exists across all locales (e.g., a brand name in the navbar), grep for that. Don't grep for locale-specific text unless you know which locale the root redirect settles on.
- **IPv6/IPv4 resolution trap (`localhost` vs `127.0.0.1`)**: On Debian VPS cloud images, `/etc/hosts` typically has `::1 localhost` but may NOT have `127.0.0.1 localhost`. Python's aiohttp uses happy-eyeballs (tries IPv6 first) — if the target service only listens on IPv4, `curl localhost:3000` works fine (curl prefers IPv4) but `aiohttp.ClientSession.get("http://localhost:3000")` fails with `Connect call failed ('::1', 3000, 0, 0)`. **Fix options:**
  1. Add `127.0.0.1 localhost` to `/etc/hosts` (alongside the `::1` line)
  2. Hardcode `127.0.0.1` instead of `localhost` in Python connection URLs
  3. Make the target service listen on `0.0.0.0` instead of `127.0.0.1` so it accepts both IPv4 and IPv6
- **Dogfood: healthcheck auto-remediation can hit IPv6 traps**: When a cron job checks `https://site.com/api/health` via `curl -sL`, curl resolves IPv4 by default and works fine. But if the healthcheck wraps Python's aiohttp for JSON parsing (e.g., `python3 -c "import json, sys; ..."` piped from curl), the `localhost` resolution may fail if the script later connects to a local service. Always prefer `127.0.0.1` over `localhost` in Python healthcheck scripts or connection tools.

### Browser vs Server Divergence (Redirect Loops)

A user reports `ERR_TOO_MANY_REDIRECTS` in the browser, but `curl -sI` from the server returns 200 or 401 with no redirects. This means the redirect loop is **browser-side** — typically a stale cached 301/302, corrupted session cookie, or a service worker intercepting the request.

**How to tell:**
```bash
# Server-side test (bypasses browser cache entirely)
curl -sI -H "Cache-Control: no-cache" https://example.com/api/health
# Returns 200 or 401 → server is fine, browser cache is the culprit

# Browser-side test
# Open DevTools → Network → check "Disable cache" and reload
# If it works with cache disabled, the issue is cached
```

**Common causes:**
- Stale service worker (`sw.js`) caching an old redirect response
- Corrupted session cookie causing auth middleware to loop between `/login` and `/`
- Cached 301 (permanent redirect) that was valid before a deploy but now points to a route that itself redirects

**Fix:**
1. Hard reload: `Ctrl+Shift+R`
2. Unregister service worker: DevTools → Application → Service Workers
3. Clear site data: DevTools → Application → Storage → Clear site data
4. Test in incognito/private window

**Healthcheck implication:** Standard cron healthchecks (curl-based) will NOT catch browser-side redirect loops. If the issue is reported by users but healthchecks are green, suspect browser cache or cookie state rather than infrastructure.

### 14. Multi-Site Batch Monitor (24+ Sites)

For monitoring a portfolio of client sites (20+), use a batch approach that's silent when healthy and only reports failures:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
SITES=(
  "https://client1.com"
  "https://client2.com"
  "https://client3.com"
  # ... all 24+ sites
)

DOWN=()
for site in "${SITES[@]}"; do
  code=$(curl -sL -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" --max-time 10 "$site" 2>/dev/null)
  if [[ "$code" != "200" && "$code" != "301" && "$code" != "302" ]]; then
    DOWN+=("$site ($code)")
  fi
done

if [ ${#DOWN[@]} -gt 0 ]; then
  echo "Sites Down:"
  for d in "${DOWN[@]}"; do echo "  $d"; done
  exit 1
fi
# Silent exit 0 — cron platform (WhatsApp) only sends on failure
```

**Key differences from single-site check:**
- No positive output — only report on failure
- 000 from curl = unreachable (likely DNS gone), not a down alert to trigger on
- Redirects (301/302) are treated as healthy since they eventually resolve
- `-L` flag to follow redirects through to final status

**DNS verification:** When sites return 000, check DNS:
```bash
dig +short <domain>
```
Empty output means no Cloudflare/any DNS record — the site name exists in config but was never deployed or the DNS was removed. Many client sites listed in READMEs are actually never-deployed and should be tracked separately from truly down sites.

**Cron integration (Silent delivery):**
- Enable `no_agent: true` so the script runs without LLM overhead
- Wire delivery to WhatsApp so you see failures immediately
- The cron job's `last_status` + `last_error` is enough to detect failures
- Exit code convention: 0 = all up (silent), 1+ = failures reported

## When to Skip `-L`

Only skip `-L` when you explicitly need to test a specific redirect (e.g., verify that `http://` → `https://` works, or that `/old-path` → `/new-path` returns the expected 301). In that case:
```bash
# Test that /old-path returns exactly 301 to /new-path
REDIRECT_CODE=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" --max-time $TIMEOUT "http://example.com/old-path")
REDIRECT_URL=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{redirect_url}" --max-time $TIMEOUT "http://example.com/old-path")
```

## Multi-Endpoint Healthcheck

For sites with multiple critical pages, check each one:

```bash
ENDPOINTS=("/es/" "/en/" "/api/health")
FAILED=0

for path in "${ENDPOINTS[@]}"; do
  STATUS=$(curl -sL -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" --max-time $TIMEOUT "${URL}${path}")
  if [ "$STATUS" != "200" ]; then
    echo "DOWN|${URL}${path} returned $STATUS"
    FAILED=1
  fi
done

[ "$FAILED" -eq 0 ] && echo "OK|All endpoints healthy" && exit 0
exit 1
```

## SSL Check

Optionally verify SSL expiry as a secondary check (weekly, not every run):

```bash
EXPIRY=$(echo | openssl s_client -servername "$HOST" -connect "$HOST:443" 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -enddate)
echo "$EXPIRY"  # Output: notAfter=Jul 20 18:28:09 2026 GMT
```

## Cron Integration

Healthcheck scripts should be small, fast, and stateless — they run on a cron schedule and exit immediately. The cron job's `last_status` field captures the exit code.

For Hermes cron jobs using a `script` field, the script's exit code is tracked:
- Exit 0 → `ok`
- Exit 1 → error (logged in `last_error`)
- Exit 2+ → error (logged in `last_error`)

## Auto-Remediation on Healthcheck Failure

For critical services, combine healthcheck + auto-remediation in the same cron job.
When the health endpoint returns non-ok, force-restart the Docker service:

```bash
# Cron job pattern: check health, auto-remediate on failure
CHECK_URL="${1:-https://example.com/api/health}"
SERVICE_NAME="${2:-stack_name_service}"

# Fetch and parse JSON health response
HEALTH=$(curl -sL --max-time 10 "$CHECK_URL" 2>/dev/null)

if echo "$HEALTH" | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); sys.exit(0 if d.get('status')=='ok' else 1)" 2>/dev/null; then
  echo "HEALTHY|$CHECK_URL"
  exit 0
fi

echo "DOWN|$CHECK_URL — forcing service restart..."
cd /root/stack-dir && docker service update --force "$SERVICE_NAME"
echo "RECOVERED|$SERVICE_NAME force-updated"
exit 0  # Exit 0 because we recovered
```

**Key design choices:**
- Exit 0 on recovery too — the cron system only alerts on non-zero exit. Once remediated, alerting would be noise.
- `python3` inline for JSON parsing avoids fragility of `grep` on JSON
- Hardcoded paths (`cd /root/stack-dir`) since cron jobs are per-environment
- The service update is forced (`--force`) to trigger a rolling restart even if the image is the same

**Pitfall — circular restarts:** If the service keeps failing, each cron run will force-restart it, producing a log spike. Add a rate limit:
```bash
# Only restart if last restart was > 2 minutes ago
LAST_RESTART=$(docker service inspect "$SERVICE_NAME" --format '{{.UpdatedAt}}' 2>/dev/null)
# Parse and compare timestamp, skip if too recent
```

---

# Hermes Workspace Deployment

## Architecture

```
VPS
├── Gateway (8642)         — hermes gateway run → bound 127.0.0.1:8642
├── Dashboard (9119)       — hermes dashboard --port 9119 --host 0.0.0.0
├── Workspace (Docker 8789) — ghcr.io/outsourc-e/hermes-workspace
├── Proxy (8788)           — /opt/hermes-workspace-proxy.py (systemd)
├── Cloudflared Tunnel     — routes webui.paragu-ai.com → 127.0.0.1:8788
└── Webhook Server (8644)

Cloudflare Edge
└── webui.paragu-ai.com → DNS proxy → Tunnel → 8788 → 8789

LAPTOP
└── Brave → https://webui.paragu-ai.com
```

## Failure Mode Diagnosis

### Symptom → Root Cause → Fix

| Browser shows | Likely cause | Immediate check | Fix |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 502 Bad Gateway (Cloudflare page) | Proxy (8788) process hung in CLOSE_WAIT | `curl -sI http://127.0.0.1:8788/` — empty reply | `kill -9 $(pgrep -f hermes-workspace-proxy)` then restart |
| ERR_TOO_MANY_REDIRECTS + 500 sessions/files/skills | CLAUDE_DASHBOARD_TOKEN wrong or "disabled" | `docker exec <container> sh -c 'echo $CLAUDE_DASHBOARD_TOKEN'` | Match to dashboard's `HERMES_DASHBOARD_TOKEN` (cat /proc/<dashboard-pid>/environ) |
| 500 on sessions/files/skills but ping works | HERMES_DASHBOARD_URL pointing to invalid port | `docker service inspect hermes-ws_hermes-workspace` → check env | `docker service update --env-rm HERMES_DASHBOARD_URL hermes-ws_...` |
| 530 Cloudflare error | Tunnel connected but origin unreachable | Check cloudflared logs | Verify all services running (ports 8788, 8789, 8642, 9119) |
| 503 Service Unavailable | Gateway (8642) not reachable from Docker | `docker exec <container> curl -s http://172.17.0.1:8642/api/ping` | Gateway binds to 127.0.0.1 only — use socat bridge (see below) |
| 404 on /api/gateway/sessions or /api/cli-agents | EXPECTED — proxy filters these. If not getting 404, proxy is bypassed | Check cloudflared config points to 8788, not 8789 | Fix `~/.cloudflared/config.yml` |
| **Failed to fetch dynamically imported module** (e.g. `/assets/terminal-workspace-*.js`) | Browser/Cloudflare cached stale `index.html` referencing old chunk hashes after workspace restart | `curl -sI` on failing asset — check `cf-cache-status`; verify chunk exists in container | Patch proxy cache-control headers (see SPA Chunk Loading section below); tell user to clear browser cache |
| "Authentication error — check your API key in Settings" (workspace chat) | HERMES_API_TOKEN doesn't match gateway's API_SERVER_KEY | Check workspace env `HERMES_API_TOKEN` vs gateway's `API_SERVER_KEY` | Match tokens OR leave API_SERVER_KEY empty (localhost-only) |

## Proxy Layer

File: `/opt/hermes-workspace-proxy.py`

The proxy:
- Listens on 8788
- Forwards everything to 8789 (workspace)
- Catches `/api/gateway/sessions` → 404
- Catches `/api/cli-agents` → 404
- Handles WebSocket upgrades for `/api/terminal-stream` (rewrite: bidirectional TCP tunnel using `select`)

**Common failure**: proxy process enters CLOSE_WAIT state (SIGTERM doesn't kill it — need SIGKILL). Install as systemd service with `Restart=always`.

**WebSocket support**: The original proxy used `urllib.request.urlopen` which doesn't handle WebSocket. Rewritten to detect `Upgrade: websocket` headers and create a raw TCP tunnel using `select.select` for bidirectional frame forwarding.

```bash
systemctl status hermes-workspace-proxy
systemctl restart hermes-workspace-proxy  # may hang if process stuck
kill -9 $(pgrep -f hermes-workspace-proxy)
```

**Verify proxy is working:**
```bash
curl -sI http://127.0.0.1:8788/ 2>&1 | head -3
# Expected: HTTP/1.0 200 OK

## Auth Token Chain — Two Separate Systems

The workspace uses **two separate tokens** for two different backends:

| Token env var | Used for | Must match |
|---|---|---|
| `CLAUDE_DASHBOARD_TOKEN` | Dashboard API (sessions, files, skills) | Dashboard's `HERMES_DASHBOARD_TOKEN` (process env) |
| `HERMES_API_TOKEN` | Gateway API (chat completions, models) | Gateway's `API_SERVER_KEY` or empty (localhost-only) |

Workspace code:
```javascript
// Gateway auth
const BEARER_TOKEN = process.env.HERMES_API_TOKEN || process.env.CLAUDE_API_TOKEN || "";
// Dashboard auth
const DASHBOARD_BEARER_TOKEN = process.env.CLAUDE_DASHBOARD_TOKEN || process.env.CLAUDE_DASHBOARD_TOKEN || "";
```

**To find the correct dashboard token:**
```bash
DASH_PID=$(ss -tlnp | grep 9119 | grep -oP 'pid=\\K[0-9]+')
cat /proc/$DASH_PID/environ | tr '\\0' '\\n' | grep HERMES_DASHBOARD_TOKEN

# Update workspace to match
docker service update \
  --env-rm CLAUDE_DASHBOARD_TOKEN \
  --env-add CLAUDE_DASHBOARD_TOKEN=<dashboard-token> \
  hermes-ws_hermes-workspace
```

**Gateway auth** (v1/models, v1/chat/completions):

The gateway validates incoming requests against `API_SERVER_KEY`:
```python
self._api_key = extra.get("key", os.getenv("API_SERVER_KEY", ""))
if not self._api_key:
    return None  # No key → allow all (localhost-only)
```

Fix gateway auth:
- **Option A** (no auth needed): Leave `API_SERVER_KEY` unset. Gateway allows all requests. Safe because it binds to `127.0.0.1:8642` (localhost only).
- **Option B** (with auth): Add `API_SERVER_KEY=...` to `/root/.hermes/.env` file, or add `key: <value>` under `gateway.platforms.api_server` in config.yaml.
- **IMPORTANT**: `source venv/bin/activate` UNSETS the `API_SERVER_KEY` env var. If starting gateway via `bash -c 'source venv/bin/activate && python ...'`, the env var is lost. Use the `.env` file or config.yaml instead.
- **VERIFY**: `curl -s http://127.0.0.1:8642/v1/models` — returns model list without auth when no key set.

**Common misconfigurations:**
- `CLAUDE_DASHBOARD_TOKEN=disabled` — breaks all dashboard API calls silently (workspace probe passes, runtime calls fail with 500)
- `HERMES_API_TOKEN` set to DeepSeek API key instead of gateway's `API_SERVER_KEY` — causes "Authentication error — check your API key in Settings" in workspace chat

## Env Var Conflicts

**Key insight**: Docker Swarm env vars can OVERRIDE `workspace-overrides.json` values. If the env var is set, the workspace code reads it before the override file.

Always check BOTH:
```bash
docker service inspect hermes-ws_hermes-workspace --format '{{range .Spec.TaskTemplate.ContainerSpec.Env}}{{$v}}{{"\n"}}{{end}}'
# AND
cat /opt/hermes-workspace-data/workspace-overrides.json
```

**Notorious env vars that cause issues:**
- `HERMES_DASHBOARD_URL=http://127.0.0.1:1` (port 1 doesn't exist → remove with `--env-rm`)
- `HERMES_DASHBOARD_URL=http://172.17.0.1:9119` vs override's `claudeDashboardUrl` — prefer override

## Socat Bridge for Gateway

Gateway binds to `127.0.0.1:8642` by default (hardcoded — no --host flag). Docker containers can't reach this via `172.17.0.1:8642`.

**Fix**: Run socat to forward on a different port:

```bash
socat TCP-LISTEN:18642,bind=172.17.0.1,reuseaddr,fork TCP:127.0.0.1:8642 &
```

**IMPORTANT pitfall**: Socat DIES when the gateway process restarts (the TCP connection to 127.0.0.1:8642 drops and socat doesn't reconnect). After restarting the gateway, ALWAYS also restart socat:
```bash
kill $(pgrep -f "socat.*18642") 2>/dev/null
socat TCP-LISTEN:18642,bind=172.17.0.1,reuseaddr,fork TCP:127.0.0.1:8642 &
```

Then update workspace-overrides.json:
```json
{"claudeApiUrl": "http://172.17.0.1:18642", "claudeDashboardUrl": "http://172.17.0.1:9119"}
```

**Verify from container:**
```bash
docker exec <container> sh -c 'timeout 3 curl -s http://172.17.0.1:18642/v1/models'
# Expected: JSON model list (may require Bearer auth or no auth)

## Cloudflared Tunnel Config

Two config files exist. The tunnel may read either:
- `/etc/cloudflared/config.yml` (when started as systemd service)
- `~/.cloudflared/config.yml` (when started with `cloudflared tunnel run`)

**Always keep both in sync**:
```yaml
tunnel: <tunnel-id>
credentials-file: /root/.cloudflared/<tunnel-id>.json
ingress:
  - hostname: webui.paragu-ai.com
    service: http://127.0.0.1:8788   # ← PROXY, not direct to 8789
  - hostname: chat.paragu-ai.com
    service: http://127.0.0.1:9119
  - service: http_status:404
```

## Full Restart Procedure

Shutdown order (reverse of startup):
```bash
# 1. Cloudflared
kill $(pgrep -f cloudflared)
# 2. Proxy
kill -9 $(pgrep -f hermes-workspace-proxy)
# 3. Workspace
docker service scale hermes-ws_hermes-workspace=0
# 4. Dashboard
kill $(pgrep -f "hermes dashboard")
# 5. Gateway
kill $(pgrep -f "gateway run")
```

Startup order:
```bash
# 1. Gateway (in screen to survive terminal)
screen -dmS gw bash -c 'cd /root/.hermes/hermes-agent && source venv/bin/activate && python -m hermes_cli.main gateway run --replace'
sleep 30  # Gateway takes ~30s to init (MCP servers)
# 2. Dashboard
screen -dmS dash bash -c 'cd /root/.hermes/hermes-agent && source venv/bin/activate && hermes dashboard --port 9119 --host 0.0.0.0 --insecure --no-open --skip-build'
# 3. Workspace
docker service scale hermes-ws_hermes-workspace=1
# Wait for workspace container to start
sleep 3
# 4. Proxy (systemd)
systemctl start hermes-workspace-proxy
# 5. Socat bridge — MUST restart after gateway
kill $(pgrep -f "socat.*18642") 2>/dev/null
socat TCP-LISTEN:18642,bind=172.17.0.1,reuseaddr,fork TCP:127.0.0.1:8642 &
# 6. Cloudflared
screen -dmS cf cloudflared tunnel run
```

## Verification

```bash
echo -n "Gateway 8642: "; ss -tlnp | grep -q 8642 && echo "OK" || echo "FAIL"
echo -n "Dashboard 9119: "; ss -tlnp | grep -q 9119 && echo "OK" || echo "FAIL"
echo -n "Proxy 8788: "; ss -tlnp | grep -q 8788 && echo "OK" || echo "FAIL"
echo -n "Workspace 8789: "; ss -tlnp | grep -q 8789 && echo "OK" || echo "FAIL"
echo -n "Socat 18642: "; ss -tlnp | grep -q 18642 && echo "OK" || echo "FAIL"

curl -s -o /dev/null -w "HTTPS: %{http_code}\n" --max-time 10 https://webui.paragu-ai.com/

# Check workspace detected all capabilities
docker logs $(docker ps --filter "name=hermes-ws_hermes-workspace" --format '{{.Names}}' | head -1) --tail 5
# Expected: core=[health, chatCompletions, models, streaming, dashboard] enhanced=[sessions, skills, memory, config, jobs]
```

## SPA Chunk Loading Failure After Deployment

The workspace is a Vite-built SPA. After container restart, hashed chunk filenames change. If `index.html` is cached (browser or Cloudflare edge), the browser tries to load old chunks → 404 → "Failed to fetch dynamically imported module."

**Quick diagnosis:**
```bash
# Verify chunk exists in current container
docker exec <workspace-container> ls -la /app/dist/client/assets/ | grep terminal-workspace
# If file exists → caching issue, not missing file

# Check Cloudflare cache status
curl -sI https://webui.paragu-ai.com/assets/terminal-workspace-kHpbSPuZ.js | grep -i cf-cache-status
```

**Proxy fix** (prevents recurrence — see `references/[REDACTED_PHONE]-spa-chunk-loading-failure.md` for full details):
- HTML → `Cache-Control: no-cache, no-store, must-revalidate`
- Assets → `Cache-Control: public, max-age=60, must-revalidate` + `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *`

**Immediate client workaround:**
- Android Chrome: Clear cached data for `webui.paragu-ai.com`
- Or open in **Incognito** (bypasses local cache)

## Pitfalls

- **SIGTERM doesn't kill hung proxy** — always use `kill -9`.
- **Two cloudflared configs** — `/etc/` and `~/.cloudflared/` may diverge. Both must point to 8788.
- **Docker service update can be blocked** by terminal safety checks. Use background mode for `docker service update --force`.
- **Gateway `tcsetattr: Inappropriate ioctl for device`** — happens when started in non-TTY context. Use `screen` or `background=true`.
- **Workspace restart clears browser session** — user needs to re-enter password after container recreation.
- **Env vars take precedence over workspace-overrides.json** — always check `docker service inspect` for errant env vars.
- **CLAUDE_DASHBOARD_TOKEN=disabled** — common misconfiguration that breaks all dashboard API calls silently (workspace startup probe passes but runtime calls fail with 500).
- **`source venv/bin/activate` clears env vars** — `API_SERVER_KEY` and other env vars set before sourcing the venv activate script are lost. Set them in `/root/.hermes/.env` or config.yaml instead.
- **Socat dies on gateway restart** — always restart socat after killing/recreating the gateway process. The TCP connection to 127.0.0.1:8642 drops and socat doesn't auto-reconnect.
- **Cloudflare error 530** — means tunnel edge can connect but origin (proxy/workplace) is unreachable. Check that 8788/8789 are listening.
- **SPA chunk loading failure after deployment** — browser or Cloudflare caches old `index.html` referencing dead chunk hashes. Patch proxy cache-control headers and tell user to clear cache. See `references/[REDACTED_PHONE]-spa-chunk-loading-failure.md`.
